title: Plant Sunlight Hours Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Sunlight Hours Your Plants Need
description: Find recommended sunlight hours for your plants by type. Free instant calculator with recommendations.
intro: The Plant Sunlight Hours Calculator tells you exactly how many hours of direct or indirect sunlight your plant needs each day to thrive. Every plant species belongs to one of three core light categories — full sun (6–8+ hours of direct sunlight), partial sun/shade (3–6 hours), or full shade (fewer than 3 hours) — and matching your plant to the right category is the single most controllable factor in its survival and yield. Use this tool when placing plants in a new garden bed, choosing a window for an indoor plant, or troubleshooting leggy, yellowing, or stunted growth caused by light imbalance.
keyTakeaway: **Daily Sunlight Requirement = Plant Light Category × Season Adjustment Factor** — e.g., a full-sun tomato needs **6–8 h direct sun/day** in summer, while a shade fern thrives on **1–2 h of indirect light/day**.
useCases: Determining the best garden bed location for tomatoes, peppers, or squash that need 6–8 hours of direct summer sun to set fruit properly.
Choosing the right windowsill (south-facing vs. north-facing) for indoor plants like pothos, snake plants, or orchids based on their light tolerance.
Diagnosing why a houseplant is growing leggy, losing color, or dropping leaves — symptoms directly tied to receiving too few or too many sunlight hours.
Planning a vegetable garden layout by mapping which yard zones receive full sun, partial shade, or full shade throughout the day using daily hour counts.
Selecting companion plants for a mixed garden bed where taller crops like corn cast shade, reducing available hours for shorter neighbors like lettuce.
Adjusting grow-light schedules (in hours per day) for indoor seedlings or hydroponic setups to replicate natural sunlight requirements.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

Plant sunlight requirements are expressed in **daily hours of photosynthetically active radiation (PAR)** received at the plant's canopy. The core classification system used by horticulturalists and the USDA is:

```
Light Category        | Direct Sun Hours/Day
----------------------|---------------------
Full Sun              | ≥ 6 hours (ideally 6–8+)
Partial Sun           | 3–6 hours direct sun
Partial Shade         | 2–4 hours direct sun + filtered light
Full Shade            | < 2 hours direct / no direct sun
Deep Shade            | Reflected or ambient light only
```

**Season Adjustment Formula:**

When the sun is lower in the sky (fall/winter), effective PAR hours drop even if the clock hours of daylight remain similar. A rough adjustment factor:

```
Effective Sun Hours = Daylight Hours × Sun Angle Factor
Sun Angle Factor (summer, latitude 35–45°N) ≈ 0.75–0.85
Sun Angle Factor (winter, latitude 35–45°N) ≈ 0.40–0.55
```

So a south-facing bed that gets 8 clock hours of sun exposure in July may deliver only ~4 effective hours in December — pushing a "full sun" perennial into "partial sun" conditions.

---

## Reference Table

The following daily sunlight requirements are based on USDA and cooperative extension guidelines:

| Plant                  | Light Category   | Min Hours/Day | Ideal Hours/Day | Notes                                 |
|------------------------|------------------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------------------------|
| Tomato                 | Full Sun         | 6             | 7–8             | < 6 h reduces fruit set significantly |
| Pepper                 | Full Sun         | 6             | 8               | Needs heat + light for ripening       |
| Zucchini / Squash      | Full Sun         | 6             | 7–8             | Powdery mildew risk in shade           |
| Basil                  | Full Sun         | 6             | 6–8             | Bolts faster with excess heat/light   |
| Lettuce                | Partial Shade    | 3             | 4–6             | Bolts in full summer sun              |
| Spinach                | Partial Shade    | 3             | 4–5             | Prefers cooler, filtered light        |
| Rose                   | Full Sun         | 5             | 6–8             | Disease risk increases below 5 h      |
| Lavender               | Full Sun         | 6             | 8+              | Native to Mediterranean climates      |
| Fern (outdoor)         | Full Shade       | 0–1           | 1–2 indirect    | Burns in direct sun                   |
| Hostas                 | Full Shade       | 0–1           | 1–3 indirect    | Variegated types tolerate more light  |
| Pothos (indoor)        | Low–Medium Light | 2–4 indirect  | 4–6 indirect    | Tolerates fluorescent light           |
| Snake Plant            | Low Light        | 2 indirect    | 4–6 indirect    | Survives, not thrives, in low light   |
| Orchid (Phalaenopsis)  | Bright Indirect  | 4–6 indirect  | 6 indirect      | No direct sun — burns leaves          |
| Peace Lily             | Low–Medium       | 2–4 indirect  | 4–5 indirect    | One of the lowest-light tolerant      |
| Succulents / Cacti     | Full Sun         | 6             | 8–12            | South-facing window or outdoors       |
| Strawberry             | Full Sun         | 6             | 8–10            | Fruit production drops below 6 h      |
| Blueberry              | Full Sun         | 6             | 8               | Requires acidic soil + full sun       |
| Mint                   | Partial Sun      | 4             | 4–6             | One of few herbs tolerant of shade    |

---

## Typical Cases

**Case 1 — Tomatoes on a north-facing patio (problem scenario):**
A gardener places tomato plants on a north-facing balcony that receives 4 hours of sun per day. Tomatoes require a minimum of 6 hours. Result: plants flower weakly, fruit set is poor, blossom-end rot risk increases. Fix: move to a south- or west-facing location, or supplement with a 300–600W full-spectrum grow light for 3–4 additional hours daily.

**Case 2 — Fern installed in a south-facing window (problem scenario):**
An outdoor fern variety is placed on a south-facing windowsill receiving 7 hours of direct sun. Ferns evolved for forest understory conditions (< 2 h indirect light). Leaf tips brown and crisp within 1–2 weeks due to photobleaching. Fix: move to a north-facing window or use a sheer curtain to diffuse light.

**Case 3 — Indoor herb garden under grow lights:**
A gardener grows basil indoors under a full-spectrum LED panel rated at ~50 µmol/m²/s PAR. Natural sunlight at noon delivers ~1,500–2,000 µmol/m²/s. To compensate, the grow light must run **16–18 hours/day** to approximate the DLI (Daily Light Integral) of 6–8 outdoor sun hours. Formula: `DLI (mol/m²/day) = PPFD (µmol/m²/s) × hours × 0.0036`; target DLI for basil ≈ 12–17 mol/m²/day.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Counting total daylight, not direct sun hours** — A garden bed may have 12 hours of daylight but only 5 hours of unobstructed direct sunlight due to fences, trees, and buildings. Always measure the *direct exposure window*, not sunrise-to-sunset.

2. **Ignoring seasonal sun angle changes** — A spot that is "full sun" in June (sun angle ~70° at 40°N) can drop to partial shade in September (sun angle ~45°) as the sun tracks lower. Plan for the *growing season average*, not the summer solstice peak.

3. **Treating "bright indirect light" as equivalent to "partial sun"** — Outdoors, partial sun means 3–6 hours of direct solar radiation. Indoors, "bright indirect light" near a window may deliver only 500–1,000 lux, far below outdoor partial sun (~10,000–25,000 lux). These are not interchangeable categories.

4. **Placing shade plants near heat-reflecting walls** — A north-facing wall that shades the ground may still radiate significant heat and reflect ambient light, creating a micro-environment that stresses shade plants even without direct sunlight. Light hours alone don't tell the whole story — heat load matters too.

5. **Neglecting canopy competition over time** — Newly planted full-sun vegetables may receive adequate light in spring, but as neighboring tomatoes or corn grow to 4–6 feet tall by mid-summer, they cast shadows that reduce hours for shorter companions like peppers or herbs.

---

## Related Calculators

- Explore companion planting needs with our [Garden Spacing Calculator](/garden-spacing)
- Plan your irrigation around sunlight with the [Plant Watering Calculator](/plant-watering)
- Optimize indoor setups with the [Grow Light Coverage Calculator](/grow-light-coverage)

faq.q: How many hours of sunlight does a tomato plant need per day?
faq.a: Tomatoes require a minimum of 6 hours of direct sunlight per day, with 7–8 hours being the ideal range for strong fruit set. Fewer than 6 hours leads to weak flowering, poor pollination, and significantly reduced yields. USDA cooperative extension guides consistently place tomatoes in the 'full sun' category, meaning unobstructed direct sun — not filtered light through a sheer curtain.

faq.q: What is the difference between full sun, partial sun, and full shade?
faq.a: These are standardized horticultural categories: Full Sun = 6 or more hours of direct sunlight per day; Partial Sun = 3–6 hours of direct sun; Partial Shade = 2–4 hours of direct sun plus additional filtered/ambient light; Full Shade = fewer than 2 hours of direct sun, primarily ambient or reflected light. The USDA and most nursery labeling systems use this scale, so it appears directly on plant tags.

faq.q: Can I use a grow light to replace natural sunlight hours?
faq.a: Yes, but you must account for the lower intensity. A typical residential full-spectrum LED grow light delivers 50–200 µmol/m²/s PPFD, while direct midday sun provides ~1,500–2,000 µmol/m²/s. To hit the same Daily Light Integral (DLI) as 6 outdoor sun hours, a low-intensity grow light may need to run 16–18 hours per day. The formula is: DLI = PPFD × hours × 0.0036, with target DLI values ranging from ~6 mol/m²/day for shade plants to 20+ for full-sun crops.

faq.q: Does the direction my window faces affect how many sunlight hours my indoor plant receives?
faq.a: Absolutely. In the Northern Hemisphere: South-facing windows receive the most direct sun (up to 6–8 hours in summer); East-facing windows get 3–4 hours of gentler morning sun; West-facing windows get 3–4 hours of stronger afternoon sun; North-facing windows receive little to no direct sunlight and suit only low-light plants like pothos or ZZ plants. Window direction is one of the primary factors for indoor plant placement decisions.

faq.q: Why is my plant getting enough hours of light but still growing leggy (etiolated)?
faq.a: Etiolation — long, stretched stems growing toward a light source — occurs when a plant receives insufficient light intensity, not just insufficient hours. A plant near a window may get 6 clock-hours of light exposure, but if it's 6–8 feet from the glass, light intensity drops by ~75% due to the inverse square law. Move the plant within 1–2 feet of the window, or supplement with a grow light. Common culprits include pothos, succulents, and seedlings indoors.

faq.q: Do sunlight requirements change with the seasons?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. The sun's angle relative to Earth changes throughout the year, affecting both intensity and the number of effective sun hours a location receives. At 40°N latitude (e.g., Denver, Philadelphia), the solar noon angle is ~73° in June but only ~26° in December. This lower angle means sunlight passes through more atmosphere, reducing PAR delivery. A location that qualifies as 'full sun' in summer may deliver only 'partial sun' equivalent in early spring or fall — important for cool-season crops.

faq.q: What happens if a shade-loving plant gets too many hours of direct sun?
faq.a: Excess direct sunlight causes photobleaching, sunscald, and leaf tip burn in shade-adapted plants. Chlorophyll degrades faster than it can be synthesized, turning leaves pale yellow or white. In severe cases, brown, papery patches appear on leaves — analogous to a sunburn. Ferns, hostas, peace lilies, and most tropical foliage plants (which evolved under forest canopies with < 5% full-sun intensity) are particularly vulnerable. Moving the plant to filtered or indirect light usually reverses early symptoms within 1–2 weeks.

faq.q: How do I measure how many sunlight hours a specific spot in my yard actually receives?
faq.a: The most accurate low-cost method is to use a sunlight meter (solar pathfinder or a dedicated lux meter) placed at plant-canopy height in the spot you want to measure. Observe and record whether the location receives direct, unobstructed sun at each hour of the day, then count the hours. A simple free method: check the spot every 30–60 minutes on a clear day and note when direct sun is present. For precision, affordable light-hours meters (e.g., Luster Leaf Rapitest) are available for under $15 and accumulate daily readings automatically.
